learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, HashMap, Loops, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine a classroom attendance sheet where some students appear multiple times because they attended different sessions. The student with the highest attendance determines the degree of the class. Now you need to find the shortest continuous attendance record that still contains that maximum attendance count.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine a classroom attendance sheet where some students appear multiple times because they attended different sessions. The student with the highest attendance determines the degree of the class. Now you need to find the shortest continuous attendance record that still contains that maximum attendance count.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this HashMap frequency tracking pattern whenever you need both the frequency of elements and their positions in the array.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks for both the frequency and position of elements, think of maintaining frequency and index HashMaps together.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given a non-empty array of non-negative integers nums, the degree of this array is defined as the maximum frequency of any one of its elements. Return the length of the smallest possible contiguous subarray that has the same degree as nums.

examples.explanation: The degree is 2. Number 2 forms the shortest subarray [2,2] of length 2.
The degree is 3. The shortest subarray containing all three occurrences of 2 has length 6.

edgeCases: Multiple numbers with the same degree

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every possible subarray, calculate its degree, and compare it with the degree of the original array.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: To determine the shortest valid subarray, you need three pieces of information for every number: how many times it appears, where it first appeared, and where it last appeared.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Only numbers with the maximum frequency need to be considered. Their first and last positions determine the shortest valid subarray.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks for both the frequency and position of elements, think of maintaining frequency and index HashMaps together.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element is processed exactly once. HashMap operations take O(1) average time, resulting in an overall linear-time solution.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks for both the frequency and position of elements, think of maintaining frequency and index HashMaps together.
commonWrongPaths: Updating the first occurrence multiple times.
Ignoring multiple numbers with the same degree.
Returning the frequency instead of the subarray length.

hints: Store the first occurrence of every number.
Count the frequency of every number.
Track the last occurrence while traversing.
commonMisconceptions: Updating the first occurrence multiple times.
Ignoring multiple numbers with the same degree.
Returning the frequency instead of the subarray length.

pattern.name: Frequency Counting
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks for both the frequency and position of elements, think of maintaining frequency and index HashMaps together.

approaches.title: Check Every Subarray
approaches.idea: Generate every possible subarray, calculate its degree, and compare it with the degree of the original array.
algorithm: Calculate the degree of the original array.
Return the smallest valid subarray length.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,2,2,3,1]
Check every subarray until one with degree 2 is found.

code.java: Brute force omitted because it requires checking every subarray.
code.python: Brute force omitted because of cubic complexity.

approaches.title: Three HashMaps
approaches.idea: Store frequency, first occurrence, and last occurrence of every number using HashMaps.
approaches.dryRun:
1 -> freq=2 first=0 last=4
2 -> freq=2 first=1 last=2

approaches.title: Frequency + First and Last Occurrence
approaches.idea: Traverse the array once while maintaining three HashMaps: one for frequency, one for the first occurrence, and one for the last occurrence. After traversal, determine the maximum frequency (degree) and compute the minimum subarray length among all numbers having that degree.
approaches.intuition: Each element is processed exactly once. HashMap operations take O(1) average time, resulting in an overall linear-time solution.
algorithm: For every number with the degree, compute last - first + 1.

code.cpp: Uses three unordered_maps to store frequency, first occurrence, and last occurrence.
code.javascript: Uses three Map objects for frequency and indices.

visualization.description: Track frequency and boundary indices for every number.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved without HashMaps?
How would you return the actual subarray?
What if the array is streamed?
How would you solve this for strings?
Can the degree be updated dynamically?
interviewTips: Explain why frequency alone is insufficient.
Mention that first and last occurrences define the subarray length.
Discuss why only elements with maximum frequency matter.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Only numbers with the maximum frequency need to be considered. Their first and last positions determine the shortest valid subarray.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Frequency + First Index + Last Index → Compute Degree → Find Minimum Length.

commonMistakes: Updating the first occurrence multiple times.
Ignoring multiple numbers with the same degree.
Returning the frequency instead of the subarray length.

youtube: Nick White - Degree of an Array
relatedProblems: Find All Duplicates in an Array
